Exploring One-Variable Data.skill: 1.1 Introducing Statistics: What Can We Learn from Data?
Exploring One-Variable Data.prompt: This topic introduces statistical thinking for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Statistics is the science of collecting, analyzing, and drawing conclusions from data. Key concepts: variation is natural and expected in data; statistical methods help distinguish real patterns from random variation; data is collected to answer questions about the world; individuals are the objects described by data, and variables are characteristics measured on individuals. Types of studies: observational studies (observe without intervention) and experiments (impose treatments). Statistics provides tools for making informed decisions under uncertainty. AP exam note: students should understand that statistics is fundamentally about managing variation and making evidence-based conclusions. The investigative process (question, data, analysis, conclusion) structures statistical work. Vocabulary: statistics, data, variation, individual, variable, observational study, experiment, inference.

This topic covers types of variables for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.B, VAR-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Variables are classified as categorical (assign labels/categories, like color or gender) or quantitative (take numerical values that can be ordered and measured, like height or income). Quantitative variables are further classified as discrete (countable values, like number of siblings) or continuous (any value in an interval, like weight). Key concepts: the type of variable determines the appropriate graphs, summaries, and analyses; categorical data uses counts and proportions; quantitative data uses means, medians, and standard deviations. Students should classify variables correctly from contextual descriptions. AP exam note: correctly identifying variable type is the first step in any statistical analysis. Using the wrong methods for the variable type leads to meaningless results. Vocabulary: categorical variable, quantitative variable, discrete, continuous, classify, numerical, proportion, count.

Exploring One-Variable Data.skill: 1.3 Representing a Categorical Variable with Tables
Exploring One-Variable Data.prompt: This topic covers frequency and relative frequency tables for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). A frequency table shows counts of individuals in each category; a relative frequency table shows proportions (frequency/total). Key concepts: relative frequencies allow comparison between groups of different sizes; marginal frequencies are row or column totals in two-way tables; the sum of all relative frequencies equals 1. Students should construct one-way frequency tables and compute relative frequencies (proportions and percentages). Tables organize categorical data for analysis and are the basis for bar charts and other displays. AP exam note: students should be able to read, construct, and interpret frequency tables, using both counts and proportions as appropriate. Vocabulary: frequency table, relative frequency, proportion, count, category, marginal frequency, total.

Exploring One-Variable Data.skill: 1.4 Representing a Categorical Variable with Graphs
Exploring One-Variable Data.prompt: This topic covers graphical displays of categorical data for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.C, UNC-1.D, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Bar charts display frequencies or relative frequencies with bars for each category (bars should not touch, as categories are discrete). Pie charts show each category as a proportion of the whole. Key concepts: bar charts are more flexible and easier to read than pie charts; bars can be ordered by frequency (Pareto chart) for clarity; each bar's height represents frequency or relative frequency; compare distributions using side-by-side or segmented bar charts. Students should choose appropriate graphs and describe what they reveal about the distribution. AP exam note: students should construct and interpret bar charts and describe distributions of categorical variables in context. Vocabulary: bar chart, pie chart, frequency, relative frequency, distribution, Pareto chart, segmented bar chart.

Exploring One-Variable Data.skill: 1.5 Representing a Quantitative Variable with Graphs
Exploring One-Variable Data.prompt: This topic covers graphical displays of quantitative data for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.E, UNC-1.F, Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Common displays: dotplots (each value as a dot), histograms (bars showing frequency for intervals), and stemplots (stem-and-leaf displays). Key concepts: histograms group data into bins (intervals) of equal width; the y-axis shows frequency or relative frequency; bin width affects the appearance (too wide hides detail, too narrow creates noise); stemplots preserve actual data values while showing shape. Students should construct and interpret these displays. The choice of display depends on the data size and purpose. AP exam note: students should describe the distribution in terms of shape, center, spread, and unusual features (SOCS). Context is required. Vocabulary: dotplot, histogram, stemplot, bin width, frequency, shape, distribution, quantitative.

Exploring One-Variable Data.skill: 1.6 Describing the Distribution of a Quantitative Variable
Exploring One-Variable Data.prompt: This topic covers describing distributions of quantitative data for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.G, UNC-1.H, Unit 1, Topic 1.6). Students describe distributions using SOCS: Shape (symmetric, skewed left/right, unimodal/bimodal/uniform), Outliers (values far from the rest), Center (mean, median), and Spread (range, IQR, standard deviation). Key concepts: skewed right means tail extends to the right (mean > median); skewed left means tail extends left (mean < median); symmetric means approximately equal on both sides (mean ~ median); clusters and gaps are notable features. All descriptions must be in context (referring to the actual variable and units). AP exam note: a complete description addresses all four SOCS components in context. Omitting context or any SOCS component costs points on free-response questions. Vocabulary: shape, center, spread, outlier, skewed, symmetric, unimodal, bimodal, SOCS, context.

Exploring One-Variable Data.skill: 1.7 Summary Statistics for a Quantitative Variable
Exploring One-Variable Data.prompt: This topic covers summary statistics for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.I, UNC-1.J, Unit 1, Topic 1.7). Measures of center: mean (x-bar = sum/n, sensitive to outliers) and median (middle value, resistant to outliers). Measures of spread: range (max-min), interquartile range (IQR = Q3-Q1, resistant), standard deviation (s, measures average distance from mean, sensitive to outliers), variance (s^2). Key concepts: the mean and standard deviation are paired (both affected by outliers); the median and IQR are paired (both resistant); choosing resistant measures (median, IQR) is appropriate for skewed data or data with outliers; the standard deviation is zero only when all values are the same. Students should compute and interpret all measures. AP exam note: interpretations must be in context. 'The standard deviation of 3.2 inches means the heights typically vary about 3.2 inches from the mean.' Vocabulary: mean, median, range, IQR, standard deviation, variance, resistant, sensitive, outlier, center, spread.

This topic covers boxplots and other graphical summaries for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.K, UNC-1.L, Unit 1, Topic 1.8). A boxplot displays the five-number summary: minimum, Q1, median, Q3, maximum. Modified boxplots show outliers as individual points beyond the fences (Q1-1.5IQR and Q3+1.5IQR). Key concepts: the box spans the IQR (middle 50%); the line in the box is the median; whiskers extend to the smallest/largest non-outlier values; boxplots are useful for comparing distributions side by side; they show center, spread, and skewness but not shape details (modes, gaps). Students should construct and interpret boxplots and use the 1.5*IQR rule to identify outliers. AP exam note: side-by-side boxplots require comparative language (group A has a higher median than group B). Vocabulary: boxplot, five-number summary, quartile, IQR, whisker, outlier, fence, 1.5IQR rule, modified boxplot.

Exploring One-Variable Data.skill: 1.9 Comparing Distributions of a Quantitative Variable
Exploring One-Variable Data.prompt: This topic covers comparing distributions for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.M, UNC-1.N, UNC-1.O, Unit 1, Topic 1.9). When comparing two or more distributions, students should address shape, center, spread, and outliers for each, using comparative language. Key concepts: use side-by-side boxplots, back-to-back stemplots, or parallel dotplots for visual comparison; compare centers (which group has higher typical values), spreads (which is more variable), shapes (both symmetric? one skewed?), and note unusual features. All comparisons must use context and comparative words (greater than, less than, similar to). Students should avoid listing features separately without comparing. AP exam note: comparison questions require explicit comparative language. Saying 'Group A has mean 5, Group B has mean 3' without stating 'Group A has a higher mean' is incomplete. Vocabulary: compare, distribution, center, spread, shape, comparative language, side-by-side, variation, context.

This topic covers the normal distribution for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-2.A, VAR-2.B, VAR-2.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.10). The normal distribution is bell-shaped, symmetric, and characterized by mean (mu) and standard deviation (sigma). Key concepts: the empirical rule (68-95-99.7): approximately 68% of data within 1 sigma, 95% within 2 sigma, 99.7% within 3 sigma; z-scores standardize: z = (x - mu)/sigma, giving the number of standard deviations from the mean; the standard normal N(0,1) is the reference distribution; use z-tables or technology to find proportions/percentiles. Students should calculate z-scores, find proportions above/below/between values, and find values given proportions (inverse normal). Assessing normality: use normal probability plots (straight line suggests normality). AP exam note: normal distribution calculations are foundational for inference. Students must show the z-score computation and reference the normal distribution. Vocabulary: normal distribution, bell curve, mean, standard deviation, z-score, empirical rule, percentile, standard normal.

Exploring Two-Variable Data.prompt: This topic introduces the study of relationships between variables for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). When two variables are measured on the same individuals, we investigate whether and how they are related. Key concepts: association does not imply causation; lurking (confounding) variables may explain an apparent relationship; relationships can be positive (both increase), negative (one increases as the other decreases), or show no clear pattern. For two categorical variables, we examine two-way tables; for two quantitative variables, we use scatterplots. Students should formulate questions about relationships and understand the difference between association and causation. AP exam note: students must always consider alternative explanations for observed associations and avoid causal claims from observational data. Vocabulary: relationship, association, causation, lurking variable, confounding, positive association, negative association.

This topic covers two-way tables for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.P, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). A two-way table displays counts for two categorical variables simultaneously. Key concepts: rows represent one variable's categories, columns the other; joint frequencies are cell counts; marginal frequencies are row/column totals; the table allows examination of the relationship between the two variables. Students should construct two-way tables from data and read information from them. The table serves as the basis for computing conditional and joint probabilities, and for chi-square tests later in the course. AP exam note: students should be able to read and construct two-way tables, identifying joint and marginal frequencies accurately. Vocabulary: two-way table, joint frequency, marginal frequency, row variable, column variable, cell, total.

This topic covers conditional distributions and association for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.Q, UNC-1.R, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Conditional relative frequencies show the distribution of one variable given a specific category of the other: divide cell counts by the appropriate row or column total. Key concepts: if conditional distributions differ across categories, the variables are associated; if conditional distributions are the same, the variables are independent; compare conditional percentages (not raw counts) to assess association. Students should compute and display conditional distributions using segmented bar charts. Simpson's Paradox can occur when an association reverses when data is aggregated. AP exam note: students must compare conditional distributions (not marginal) to assess association. Vocabulary: conditional distribution, conditional relative frequency, association, independence, segmented bar chart, Simpson's Paradox.

Exploring Two-Variable Data.skill: 2.4 Representing the Relationship Between Two Quantitative Variables
Exploring Two-Variable Data.prompt: This topic covers scatterplots for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.S, DAT-1.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). A scatterplot displays the relationship between two quantitative variables, with the explanatory variable on the x-axis and the response variable on the y-axis. Students describe scatterplots using DOFS: Direction (positive/negative), Outliers (unusual points), Form (linear/nonlinear/clusters), and Strength (weak/moderate/strong). Key concepts: each point represents one individual; look for overall patterns and deviations; the explanatory variable potentially influences the response variable; context is essential in descriptions. AP exam note: scatterplot descriptions must address direction, form, strength, outliers, and context. Students should distinguish between the variables' roles (explanatory vs. response). Vocabulary: scatterplot, explanatory variable, response variable, direction, form, strength, outlier, linear, nonlinear.

This topic covers the correlation coefficient for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-1.B, DAT-1.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). The correlation r measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two quantitative variables. Properties: -1 <= r <= 1; r > 0 indicates positive association, r < 0 negative; |r| close to 1 means strong linear relationship, close to 0 means weak; r = +/-1 means perfect linear relationship; r has no units and is not affected by changes in units or which variable is x or y. Key concepts: r measures only linear relationships (a strong curved pattern can have r near 0); r is sensitive to outliers; r^2 gives the proportion of variability in y explained by the linear relationship with x. Students should interpret r in context. AP exam note: students must interpret r and r^2 in context and understand r's limitations (linear only, sensitive to outliers). Vocabulary: correlation, r, linear, strength, direction, r-squared, proportion of variation, outlier sensitive, unitless.

This topic covers linear regression for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-1.D, DAT-1.E, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). The least-squares regression line (LSRL) y-hat = a + bx minimizes the sum of squared residuals. Slope b = r(s_y/s_x) represents the predicted change in y for a one-unit increase in x. Intercept a = y-bar - b*x-bar is the predicted y when x = 0. Key concepts: the LSRL always passes through (x-bar, y-bar); b has units of y per x; the intercept may or may not have contextual meaning; use the regression equation for predictions within the range of data (interpolation, not extrapolation). Students should compute, interpret, and use regression equations. AP exam note: slope interpretation must include 'predicted,' units, and context: 'For each additional year, the predicted salary increases by $2,500.' Vocabulary: regression, LSRL, slope, intercept, prediction, least squares, residual, interpolation, extrapolation.

This topic covers residuals for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-1.F, DAT-1.G, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). A residual is the difference between the observed y-value and the predicted y-value: residual = y - y-hat. Positive residual means the model underestimates; negative means overestimate. Key concepts: the sum of residuals for the LSRL is always zero; residual plots (residuals vs. x or y-hat) help assess model fit; a good linear model shows a random scatter of residuals with no pattern; curved patterns in the residual plot suggest a nonlinear relationship; increasing spread suggests the variability is not constant (heteroscedasticity). Students should calculate residuals, construct residual plots, and interpret them. AP exam note: residual plot analysis is essential for assessing model appropriateness. Students must describe patterns or lack thereof in residual plots. Vocabulary: residual, observed, predicted, residual plot, pattern, random scatter, model fit, heteroscedasticity.

This topic covers properties of least squares regression for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-1.H, DAT-1.I, Unit 2, Topic 2.8). The LSRL minimizes the sum of squared residuals (SSE). Key properties: r^2 (coefficient of determination) gives the proportion of variation in y explained by the linear model with x (e.g., 'r^2 = 0.85 means 85% of the variability in y is explained by the linear relationship with x'); the standard deviation of residuals (s) measures typical prediction error; influential points (points with extreme x-values) can substantially change the regression line; outliers in the y-direction have large residuals but may not be influential. Students should interpret r^2 and s in context and understand the influence of individual points on the regression. AP exam note: interpretation of r^2 and s must be in context. Students should assess the impact of influential points. Vocabulary: least squares, r-squared, coefficient of determination, standard deviation of residuals, influential point, outlier, SSE.

This topic covers departures from linearity for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-1.J, Unit 2, Topic 2.9). When the relationship between two quantitative variables is not linear, transformations can linearize the data. Common transformations: logarithmic (log y vs. x for exponential data), power (log y vs. log x for power data), square root, or reciprocal. Key concepts: residual plots reveal nonlinearity (curved patterns); applying an appropriate transformation can produce a linear relationship in the transformed variables; the regression equation in transformed variables can be back-transformed for predictions in the original scale. Students should identify nonlinear patterns, select appropriate transformations, and interpret the transformed model. AP exam note: students should be able to use residual plots to identify nonlinearity and apply transformations to achieve linearity. Vocabulary: nonlinear, transformation, logarithmic, power, residual plot, linearize, back-transform, exponential model.

Collecting Data.skill: 3.1 Introducing Statistics: Do the Data We Collected Tell the Truth?
Collecting Data.prompt: This topic introduces data collection concepts for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). The quality of statistical conclusions depends on how data is collected. Key concepts: well-designed studies produce reliable data; poorly designed studies introduce bias that undermines conclusions; the population is the entire group of interest, the sample is the subset actually studied; the goal is to make valid inferences from sample to population. Sources of bias include selection bias (some individuals more likely to be selected), response bias (respondents give inaccurate answers), and nonresponse bias (selected individuals do not participate). Students should evaluate data collection methods critically. AP exam note: students must identify sources of bias in study designs and explain how bias affects conclusions. Vocabulary: population, sample, bias, selection bias, response bias, nonresponse bias, inference, reliability.

This topic covers planning statistical studies for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-2.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Two main study types: observational studies (observe without imposing treatments) and experiments (deliberately impose treatments on subjects). Key concepts: experiments can establish cause-and-effect; observational studies can only show association; a census measures every individual in the population (often impractical); sampling uses a subset to make inferences about the population. Well-designed samples are representative of the population. The scope of inference depends on study design: random sampling allows generalization, random assignment allows causal conclusions. AP exam note: students must distinguish between observational studies and experiments and understand the scope of inference each allows. Vocabulary: observational study, experiment, census, sample, treatment, cause and effect, association, scope of inference.

This topic covers random sampling methods for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-2.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Simple random sample (SRS): every group of n individuals has an equal chance of being selected; use a random number generator or table. Other methods: stratified random sampling (divide population into strata, SRS from each), cluster sampling (divide into clusters, randomly select entire clusters), systematic sampling (select every kth individual). Key concepts: random sampling reduces bias by giving all individuals a chance of selection; stratified sampling reduces variability by ensuring representation of important subgroups; the sampling frame (list of potential subjects) should match the population. Students should describe sampling methods and identify potential flaws. AP exam note: students must describe how to implement a specific sampling method, including the mechanics of randomization. Vocabulary: simple random sample, stratified, cluster, systematic, sampling frame, randomization, representative, bias.

This topic covers potential problems with sampling for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-2.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Key issues: undercoverage (some groups systematically excluded from the sampling frame), nonresponse (selected individuals do not participate), voluntary response (individuals self-select, producing biased results), wording effects (question wording influences responses), convenience sampling (choosing easily accessible individuals, not representative). These problems introduce bias, making sample results systematically different from the population truth. Students should identify potential sampling problems in given scenarios and explain how they bias results. Key concept: no amount of analysis can fix data collected with a fundamentally flawed sampling method. AP exam note: students should explain how a specific bias affects the results (overestimates or underestimates the parameter) and suggest improvements. Vocabulary: undercoverage, nonresponse, voluntary response, convenience sample, bias, wording effects, sampling error.

This topic covers experimental design for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-3.A, VAR-3.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). An experiment imposes treatments on subjects to observe responses. Key principles: control (use a control group to compare against), randomization (randomly assign subjects to treatment groups to balance confounding variables), replication (use enough subjects to detect treatment effects). Key vocabulary: experimental units (subjects), treatments (conditions imposed), response variable (outcome measured), factors (explanatory variables), levels (specific values of factors), confounding variable (affects response and is associated with the factor). Students should identify these components in experimental descriptions. AP exam note: students must identify experimental units, treatments, response variable, and explain the role of randomization and replication. Vocabulary: experiment, treatment, control, randomization, replication, experimental unit, response variable, factor, confounding.

This topic covers types of experimental designs for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-3.C, VAR-3.D, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Common designs: completely randomized design (CRD, randomly assign all subjects to treatments), randomized block design (group subjects into blocks based on a relevant characteristic, randomize within blocks), matched pairs design (pair subjects or use subjects as their own controls, randomize treatment order within pairs). Key concepts: blocking reduces variability by accounting for known differences between subjects; matched pairs is a special case of blocking with block size 2; the choice of design depends on the variables and research question. Students should describe how to implement each design for given scenarios. AP exam note: students should explain why blocking is used (to reduce the effect of a confounding variable) and describe the randomization procedure within the chosen design. Vocabulary: completely randomized design, randomized block design, matched pairs, blocking, random assignment, reduce variability.

This topic covers the relationship between experiments and inference for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-3.E, DAT-2.D, DAT-2.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Properly designed experiments with random assignment can support causal conclusions: if treatment groups differ significantly, we can attribute the difference to the treatment rather than confounding variables. Key concepts: random assignment creates groups that are approximately equal in all characteristics except the treatment; statistically significant results (unlikely to occur by chance alone) provide evidence of a treatment effect; scope of inference depends on both sampling and assignment (random sampling allows generalization, random assignment allows causation). Students should determine what conclusions are appropriate based on study design. AP exam note: questions about scope of inference are common. Students must consider both how subjects were selected and how they were assigned to treatments. Vocabulary: causal conclusion, random assignment, statistical significance, scope of inference, generalization, treatment effect.

Probability, Random Variables, and Proba.skill: 4.1 Introducing Statistics: Random and Non-Random Patterns?
Probability, Random Variables, and Proba.prompt: This topic introduces the role of probability in statistics for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.F, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Random processes produce outcomes that are unpredictable in the short run but follow predictable patterns in the long run. Key concepts: randomness is not the same as chaos; long-run relative frequencies stabilize (law of large numbers); probability describes the long-run behavior of random processes; simulation can model random processes when theoretical analysis is difficult. Statistical inference relies on probability to determine whether observed patterns are consistent with random chance or suggest a real effect. Students should understand that probability provides the foundation for inference. AP exam note: students should distinguish between deterministic and random patterns and understand how probability underpins statistical reasoning. Vocabulary: random process, probability, long-run, relative frequency, law of large numbers, simulation, randomness, pattern.

This topic covers simulation for estimating probabilities for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-2.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). When theoretical probability is difficult to compute, simulation uses random number generation to estimate probabilities. Steps: (1) describe how to model one trial (assign random numbers to outcomes in proportion to their probabilities); (2) define what constitutes a 'success'; (3) perform many trials (typically 100+); (4) estimate the probability as the proportion of successes. Key concepts: more trials give more accurate estimates; the simulation model must match the real situation; random number tables, random digit generators, or technology can be used. Students should design, carry out, and interpret simulations. AP exam note: students must describe the simulation process clearly, including how digits/numbers correspond to outcomes and what one trial represents. Vocabulary: simulation, trial, random number, probability estimate, model, long-run proportion, success.

This topic introduces basic probability for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-4.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). The probability of an event A, P(A), is the long-run proportion of times A occurs. Key rules: 0 <= P(A) <= 1; P(sample space) = 1; P(complement of A) = 1 - P(A). The sample space S is the set of all possible outcomes. An event is a subset of the sample space. For equally likely outcomes, P(A) = (number of outcomes in A)/(total outcomes). Key concepts: probability as a long-run frequency (frequentist interpretation); the complement rule is often easier than direct computation; students should identify sample spaces and events for given random processes. AP exam note: students should express probabilities as fractions, decimals, or percentages and use correct probability notation. Vocabulary: probability, event, sample space, outcome, complement, equally likely, long-run frequency.

This topic covers mutually exclusive (disjoint) events for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-4.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Events A and B are mutually exclusive if they cannot both occur: P(A and B) = 0. The addition rule for mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). For non-mutually-exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B) (general addition rule). Key concepts: mutually exclusive events have no overlap; being mutually exclusive is a property of events, not outcomes; if events are mutually exclusive, they cannot be independent (unless one has probability 0). Students should determine whether events are mutually exclusive from context and apply the appropriate addition rule. AP exam note: students must distinguish between mutually exclusive and independent events, as these are commonly confused. Vocabulary: mutually exclusive, disjoint, addition rule, general addition rule, overlap, union, probability.

This topic covers conditional probability for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-4.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.5). The conditional probability of A given B is P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B), representing the probability of A occurring knowing B has occurred. This can be computed from two-way tables: P(A|B) = (count in A and B)/(count in B). Key concepts: conditioning reduces the sample space to only outcomes where B occurred; P(A|B) != P(B|A) in general; the multiplication rule follows: P(A and B) = P(A|B)*P(B) = P(B|A)*P(A). Tree diagrams help organize sequential probability calculations. Bayes' Theorem (not explicitly required) connects P(A|B) to P(B|A). AP exam note: students should compute conditional probabilities from tables and tree diagrams and interpret them in context. Vocabulary: conditional probability, given, reduce sample space, multiplication rule, tree diagram, two-way table.

Probability, Random Variables, and Proba.skill: 4.6 Independent Events and Unions of Events
Probability, Random Variables, and Proba.prompt: This topic covers independence and unions for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-4.D, VAR-4.E, Unit 4, Topic 4.6). Events A and B are independent if P(A|B) = P(A), equivalently P(A and B) = P(A)*P(B). Key concepts: independence means knowing one event occurred does not change the probability of the other; test independence by checking if P(A|B) = P(A) or P(A and B) = P(A)*P(B); independent does not mean mutually exclusive (these are different concepts); the multiplication rule for independent events simplifies calculations. The general addition rule gives P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B). Students should determine independence from data and compute union probabilities. AP exam note: clearly distinguish independence from mutual exclusivity. Students should justify independence using the probability definitions, not intuition. Vocabulary: independent, dependence, multiplication rule, union, intersection, general addition rule, P(A and B).

Probability, Random Variables, and Proba.skill: 4.7 Introduction to Random Variables and Probability Distributions
Probability, Random Variables, and Proba.prompt: This topic introduces random variables for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-5.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.7). A random variable X assigns a numerical value to each outcome of a random process. Discrete random variables take countable values; continuous random variables take any value in an interval. A probability distribution describes all possible values and their probabilities. For discrete X: the probabilities of all values must sum to 1, and each P(X=x) >= 0. The probability distribution can be displayed as a table, graph, or formula. Students should construct and interpret probability distributions and find probabilities of events from them. AP exam note: students should verify that a given table is a valid probability distribution (nonnegative probabilities summing to 1). Vocabulary: random variable, discrete, continuous, probability distribution, probability table, valid distribution.

Probability, Random Variables, and Proba.skill: 4.8 Mean and Standard Deviation of Random Variables
Probability, Random Variables, and Proba.prompt: This topic covers the mean and standard deviation of random variables for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-5.B, VAR-5.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.8). The mean (expected value) of discrete X: mu_X = E(X) = sum(x_i * P(x_i)). The variance: sigma^2_X = sum((x_i - mu_X)^2 * P(x_i)). Standard deviation: sigma_X = sqrt(variance). Key concepts: the mean is the long-run average value (center of the distribution); the standard deviation measures the typical deviation from the mean; the mean can be a value that X never actually takes; E(X) is a weighted average using probabilities as weights. Students should compute and interpret the mean and SD of random variables. AP exam note: interpretation must be in context: 'The expected value of $350 means that in the long run, the average payout per play is $350.' Vocabulary: expected value, mean, variance, standard deviation, weighted average, long-run average.

This topic covers combining random variables for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-5.D, VAR-5.E, Unit 4, Topic 4.9). For X and Y: E(X+Y) = E(X)+E(Y) and E(X-Y) = E(X)-E(Y) (always). For INDEPENDENT X and Y: Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y) and Var(X-Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y) (variances always add, even for differences). Linear transformations: if Y = aX+b, then E(Y) = aE(X)+b and SD(Y) = |a|SD(X). Key concepts: means add/subtract as expected; variances of independent variables always ADD (never subtract, because combining independent variables always increases overall variability); independence is required for the variance addition rule. Students should apply these rules in context. AP exam note: a common error is subtracting variances for X-Y. Students must verify independence before adding variances. Vocabulary: combining, expected value, variance, linear transformation, independent, add variances, standard deviation.

This topic introduces the binomial distribution for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-3.A, UNC-3.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.10). A binomial setting has: fixed number of trials n, two outcomes per trial (success/failure), constant probability of success p, and independent trials (BINS). The binomial random variable X counts the number of successes. P(X=k) = C(n,k)*p^k*(1-p)^(n-k). Key concepts: the binomial coefficient C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n-k)!) counts combinations; students should verify BINS conditions before applying the binomial model; the shape of the binomial distribution depends on n and p (approximately symmetric when p is near 0.5 or n is large). AP exam note: students must verify the BINS conditions and use the correct formula. Calculator notation (binompdf, binomcdf) is acceptable with parameters stated. Vocabulary: binomial distribution, BINS conditions, trial, success, failure, probability, combination, binomial coefficient.

This topic covers the mean and standard deviation of the binomial distribution for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-3.C, UNC-3.D, Unit 4, Topic 4.11). For binomial X with parameters n and p: mean mu = np, standard deviation sigma = sqrt(np(1-p)). Key concepts: the mean np gives the expected number of successes; the standard deviation sqrt(np(1-p)) measures typical variation from the mean; as n increases, the distribution becomes more symmetric and approaches a normal distribution (foundation for normal approximation in inference). Students should compute and interpret these parameters in context. For example, if n = 100 and p = 0.3, the expected number of successes is 30 with SD = sqrt(21) ~ 4.58. AP exam note: students should use these formulas rather than computing from the full probability distribution. Vocabulary: binomial mean, binomial standard deviation, np, sqrt(npq), expected number of successes, parameters.

This topic covers the geometric distribution for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-3.E, UNC-3.F, UNC-3.G, Unit 4, Topic 4.12). A geometric setting counts the number of trials until the first success: same conditions as binomial except no fixed n. P(X=k) = (1-p)^(k-1)*p for k = 1, 2, 3, .... The mean (expected number of trials) is mu = 1/p. Key concepts: the geometric distribution is memoryless: P(X > s+t | X > s) = P(X > t); the distribution is always right-skewed; P(X > k) = (1-p)^k gives the probability of no success in the first k trials. Students should verify geometric conditions, compute probabilities, and find the expected waiting time. AP exam note: students must distinguish geometric from binomial settings. Geometric counts trials until first success; binomial counts successes in fixed trials. Vocabulary: geometric distribution, first success, memoryless, expected waiting time, trials until success, right-skewed.

Sampling Distributions.skill: 5.1 Introducing Statistics: Why Is My Sample Not Like Yours?
Sampling Distributions.prompt: This topic introduces sampling variability for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.G, Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Different random samples from the same population produce different statistics (means, proportions, etc.). This natural variation is called sampling variability. Key concepts: a sampling distribution is the distribution of a statistic over all possible samples of a given size; sampling variability decreases as sample size increases; understanding sampling variability is essential for inference (it tells us how reliable our estimates are). The distinction between a parameter (fixed population value) and a statistic (calculated from a sample, varies) is fundamental. AP exam note: students should understand that sampling variability is natural and expected, and that larger samples produce less variability. Vocabulary: sampling variability, sampling distribution, parameter, statistic, sample size, variation, estimate.

This topic revisits the normal distribution in preparation for sampling distributions for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-6.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students reinforce z-score calculations (z = (x-mu)/sigma), use of normal probability tables/technology, and the relationship between areas under the normal curve and probabilities. Key concepts: the normal distribution provides the sampling distribution model for many statistics (via the Central Limit Theorem); finding P(X < a), P(X > a), P(a < X < b) using z-scores; inverse normal calculations (finding the value x given a probability). Students should be fluent with normal calculations as these are used extensively in confidence intervals and hypothesis tests. AP exam note: normal distribution calculations are used throughout Units 6-9 for inference. Students must show z-score computation and reference the normal distribution. Vocabulary: normal distribution, z-score, standard normal, area under curve, inverse normal, probability, standardize.

This topic covers the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-6.B, VAR-6.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.3). The CLT states that for sufficiently large n, the sampling distribution of x-bar is approximately normal, regardless of the population shape, with mean mu and standard deviation sigma/sqrt(n). Key concepts: the CLT is why the normal distribution is so important in statistics; it applies when n is 'large enough' (rule of thumb: n >= 30 for most populations, but depends on skewness); the standard deviation of x-bar (sigma/sqrt(n)) decreases with larger n; if the population is normal, x-bar is exactly normal for any n. Students should understand when and why the CLT applies and compute probabilities involving x-bar. AP exam note: students must verify the large sample condition when applying the CLT and understand that it justifies the use of normal-based inference procedures. Vocabulary: Central Limit Theorem, sampling distribution, approximately normal, sample size, standard error, sigma over root n.

This topic covers bias in estimation for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-3.H, Unit 5, Topic 5.4). A statistic is an unbiased estimator of a parameter if its sampling distribution is centered at the parameter value (the mean of the sampling distribution equals the parameter). Key concepts: the sample mean x-bar is unbiased for the population mean mu; the sample proportion p-hat is unbiased for the population proportion p; variability of an estimator decreases with sample size; an unbiased estimator with low variability is the most desirable (accurate and precise). Bias means the estimator systematically overestimates or underestimates the parameter. Students should distinguish between bias (systematic error) and variability (random error). AP exam note: students should understand that increasing sample size reduces variability but does not fix bias. Only improved methodology fixes bias. Vocabulary: unbiased, biased, point estimate, sampling distribution, center, variability, parameter, statistic, accurate, precise.

This topic covers the sampling distribution of p-hat for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-3.I, UNC-3.J, UNC-3.K, UNC-3.L, Unit 5, Topic 5.5). For SRS of size n from a population with proportion p: mean of p-hat = p, SD of p-hat = sqrt(p(1-p)/n). The shape is approximately normal when np >= 10 and n(1-p) >= 10 (Large Counts condition). Key concepts: p-hat is unbiased for p; the SD decreases with n (larger samples give more precise estimates); the normal approximation requires checking the Large Counts condition; the 10% condition (n < 10% of population) ensures independence when sampling without replacement. Students should compute probabilities involving p-hat using the normal approximation. AP exam note: students must check both the Large Counts and 10% conditions. These conditions reappear in inference for proportions (Units 6). Vocabulary: sample proportion, p-hat, sampling distribution, Large Counts condition, 10% condition, standard deviation, approximately normal.

Sampling Distributions.skill: 5.6 Sampling Distributions for Differences in Sample Proportions
Sampling Distributions.prompt: This topic covers the sampling distribution of p-hat_1 - p-hat_2 for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-3.M, UNC-3.N, Unit 5, Topic 5.6). For independent SRS from two populations: mean of (p-hat_1 - p-hat_2) = p_1 - p_2, SD = sqrt(p_1(1-p_1)/n_1 + p_2(1-p_2)/n_2). The shape is approximately normal when both samples satisfy the Large Counts condition (n_i*p_i >= 10 and n_i*(1-p_i) >= 10). Key concepts: the difference is unbiased for p_1 - p_2; variances add because the samples are independent; this sampling distribution is the foundation for two-proportion z-tests and confidence intervals. Students should compute probabilities for differences in proportions. AP exam note: students must verify conditions for both samples independently. Vocabulary: difference in proportions, two-sample, independent, Large Counts, standard deviation, approximately normal.

This topic covers the sampling distribution of x-bar for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-3.O, UNC-3.P, UNC-3.Q, UNC-3.R, Unit 5, Topic 5.7). For SRS of size n from a population with mean mu and SD sigma: mean of x-bar = mu, SD of x-bar = sigma/sqrt(n). Shape: if population is normal, x-bar is exactly normal for any n; otherwise, CLT gives approximate normality for large n (n >= 30 typically). Key concepts: x-bar is unbiased for mu; SD decreases with sqrt(n) (quadrupling n halves the SD); the sampling distribution forms the basis for t-procedures in inference. Students should compute probabilities for x-bar using the normal distribution. AP exam note: students must check the normality condition (population normal or n large enough via CLT) and the 10% condition. Vocabulary: sample mean, sampling distribution, Central Limit Theorem, standard error, sigma over root n, normal.

Sampling Distributions.skill: 5.8 Sampling Distributions for Differences in Sample Means
Sampling Distributions.prompt: This topic covers the sampling distribution of x-bar_1 - x-bar_2 for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-3.S, UNC-3.T, UNC-3.U, UNC-3.V, Unit 5, Topic 5.8). For independent SRS from two populations: mean = mu_1 - mu_2, SD = sqrt(sigma_1^2/n_1 + sigma_2^2/n_2). Shape is approximately normal when both populations are normal or both sample sizes are large (CLT). Key concepts: the difference is unbiased for mu_1 - mu_2; variances add for independent samples; this is the foundation for two-sample t-tests and confidence intervals. Students should check normality/CLT conditions for both samples. AP exam note: students must verify conditions for each sample and note the independence of the two samples. Vocabulary: difference in means, two-sample, independent, standard error, Central Limit Theorem, variances add.

Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.prompt: This topic introduces the rationale for normal-based inference for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.H, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Normal distributions arise naturally in sampling distributions (via CLT), making them the foundation for inference procedures. Key concepts: the sampling distribution of a statistic must be approximately normal for z-based procedures to be valid; checking conditions (Large Counts, sample size) ensures this approximation is reasonable; the normal model allows us to quantify how unusual a sample result is. This topic connects the probability foundations (Units 4-5) to the inference procedures that follow. Students should understand why checking conditions is essential before performing inference. AP exam note: always check and state conditions before performing inference procedures. Skipping conditions loses points on free-response. Vocabulary: inference, normal approximation, conditions, sampling distribution, z-procedure, Central Limit Theorem.

Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.skill: 6.2 Constructing a Confidence Interval for a Population Proportion
Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.prompt: This topic covers one-proportion z-intervals for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.A, UNC-4.B, UNC-4.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). The confidence interval for p is p-hat +/- z* * sqrt(p-hat(1-p-hat)/n). Conditions: random sample/assignment, Large Counts (n*p-hat >= 10 and n*(1-p-hat) >= 10), and 10% condition. Key concepts: the confidence level (e.g., 95%) means that if we repeated the sampling process many times, about 95% of the resulting intervals would capture the true p; z* is the critical value (1.96 for 95%); the margin of error z*sqrt(p-hat(1-p-hat)/n) quantifies precision. Students should construct and interpret confidence intervals in context. AP exam note: interpretation must say 'We are 95% confident that the true proportion of [context] is between [lower] and [upper].' NOT 'There is a 95% probability.' Vocabulary: confidence interval, margin of error, critical value, confidence level, sample proportion, interpret, conditions.

Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.skill: 6.3 Justifying a Claim Based on a Confidence Interval for a Population Proportion
Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.prompt: This topic covers using confidence intervals to justify claims for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.D, UNC-4.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). A confidence interval can be used to assess a claim about a population proportion: if the claimed value is within the interval, the data is consistent with the claim; if outside, the data provides evidence against the claim. Key concepts: a 95% CI is related to a two-sided test at alpha = 0.05; if p0 is not in the CI, we reject H0: p = p0 at the corresponding alpha level; wider intervals (higher confidence) are less likely to exclude p0. Students should use CIs to evaluate claims and understand the connection between CIs and significance tests. AP exam note: students should clearly state whether the claimed value falls inside or outside the interval and draw the appropriate conclusion. Vocabulary: confidence interval, claim, justification, reject, consistent, evidence, significance level, two-sided.

Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.skill: 6.4 Setting Up a Test for a Population Proportion
Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.prompt: This topic covers setting up a one-proportion z-test for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-6.D, VAR-6.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Steps: (1) state hypotheses: H0: p = p0 (null) and Ha: p < p0, p > p0, or p != p0 (alternative); (2) check conditions: random, Large Counts (np0 >= 10 and n(1-p0) >= 10, using p0 not p-hat), 10% condition; (3) identify the test (one-proportion z-test). Key concepts: H0 represents no effect/difference; Ha represents the claim to be tested; the significance level alpha (typically 0.05) is the threshold for evidence; one-sided vs. two-sided tests depend on the research question. Students should write hypotheses using proper notation and check conditions. AP exam note: hypotheses must use parameter notation (p, not p-hat) and be stated in context. Conditions use p0 for the Large Counts check. Vocabulary: null hypothesis, alternative hypothesis, significance level, one-sided, two-sided, conditions, z-test.

This topic covers interpreting p-values for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-3.A, DAT-3.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). The p-value is the probability of obtaining a result as extreme as (or more extreme than) the observed statistic, assuming H0 is true. Key concepts: small p-value (typically < alpha = 0.05) provides evidence against H0; large p-value means the data is consistent with H0 (but does not prove H0); the p-value is NOT the probability that H0 is true; the p-value is calculated assuming H0 is true. Students should interpret p-values in context: 'Assuming the true proportion is p0, the probability of observing a sample proportion as extreme as p-hat is [p-value].' AP exam note: correct p-value interpretation is essential. Common errors: saying p-value is 'the probability H0 is true' or 'the probability of the event occurring.' Vocabulary: p-value, extreme, assuming H0 true, evidence, significance, probability, interpretation.

Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.skill: 6.6 Concluding a Test for a Population Proportion
Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.prompt: This topic covers drawing conclusions from hypothesis tests for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-3.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Decision rule: if p-value <= alpha, reject H0 (sufficient evidence for Ha); if p-value > alpha, fail to reject H0 (insufficient evidence for Ha). Key concepts: 'fail to reject' is not the same as 'accept H0'; conclusions should be stated in context; always link the conclusion to the p-value and alpha comparison. Template: 'Since the p-value of [value] is less than alpha = 0.05, we reject H0. There is convincing evidence that [Ha in context].' Or: 'Since the p-value of [value] is greater than alpha = 0.05, we fail to reject H0. There is not convincing evidence that [Ha in context].' AP exam note: conclusions must be in context and reference the p-value/alpha comparison. Never say 'accept H0.' Vocabulary: reject, fail to reject, conclusion, context, p-value, alpha, convincing evidence, insufficient evidence.

This topic covers Type I and Type II errors and power for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-5.A, UNC-5.B, UNC-5.C, UNC-5.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.7). Type I error: rejecting H0 when H0 is true (false positive, probability = alpha). Type II error: failing to reject H0 when H0 is false (false negative, probability = beta). Power = 1 - beta = probability of correctly rejecting H0 when it is false. Key concepts: increasing alpha increases power but also increases Type I error risk; increasing n increases power and decreases both error probabilities; the effect size (how far the true parameter is from p0) affects power. Students should describe errors in context and understand the tradeoffs. AP exam note: describe errors in context: 'A Type I error would mean concluding that [Ha] when in fact [H0 is true].' Power and error relationships are commonly tested. Vocabulary: Type I error, Type II error, alpha, beta, power, false positive, false negative, sample size, effect size.

Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.skill: 6.8 Confidence Intervals for the Difference of Two Proportions
Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.prompt: This topic covers two-proportion z-intervals for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.F, UNC-4.G, UNC-4.H, Unit 6, Topic 6.8). The CI for p1-p2 is (p-hat_1 - p-hat_2) +/- z* * sqrt(p-hat_1(1-p-hat_1)/n_1 + p-hat_2(1-p-hat_2)/n_2). Conditions: random samples/assignment for both groups, Large Counts for both (n_i*p-hat_i >= 10 and n_i*(1-p-hat_i) >= 10), 10% condition for both, independence between groups. Key concepts: if 0 is in the interval, there is no significant difference; the interval estimates the true difference p1-p2. AP exam note: interpretation should reference the difference: 'We are 95% confident that the true difference in proportions is between [lower] and [upper].' Check all conditions for both groups. Vocabulary: two-proportion z-interval, difference in proportions, margin of error, conditions, independent samples, confidence.

Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.skill: 6.9 Justifying a Claim Based on a Confidence Interval for a Difference of Population Proportions
Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.prompt: This topic covers using two-proportion CIs to justify claims for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.I, UNC-4.J, Unit 6, Topic 6.9). If the CI for p1-p2 does not contain 0, there is evidence of a difference; if it contains 0, there is not convincing evidence of a difference. Key concepts: the direction of the difference matters (if the entire interval is positive, p1 > p2; if negative, p1 < p2); CIs provide more information than tests alone (they estimate the size of the difference, not just whether it exists). Students should use two-proportion CIs to evaluate claims about differences and interpret results in context. AP exam note: students should clearly state whether 0 is in the interval and draw the appropriate conclusion about the difference. Vocabulary: confidence interval, difference, claim, contains zero, evidence, direction, magnitude.

Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.skill: 6.10 Setting Up a Test for the Difference of Two Population Proportions
Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.prompt: This topic covers setting up a two-proportion z-test for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-6.F, VAR-6.G, VAR-6.H, Unit 6, Topic 6.10). Hypotheses: H0: p1 = p2 (or p1 - p2 = 0) vs. Ha: p1 < p2, p1 > p2, or p1 != p2. Under H0, use the pooled proportion p-hat_c = (X1+X2)/(n1+n2) for the standard error and condition checks. Conditions: random, Large Counts (using p-hat_c: n1*p-hat_c >= 10, n1*(1-p-hat_c) >= 10, and same for n2), 10%, independent groups. Key concepts: the pooled proportion combines both samples under the assumption H0 is true (p1 = p2); the test statistic z = (p-hat_1 - p-hat_2)/sqrt(p-hat_c(1-p-hat_c)(1/n1 + 1/n2)). AP exam note: using the pooled proportion for the test (not the CI) is important. Hypotheses use p1 and p2, not p-hat. Vocabulary: two-proportion z-test, pooled proportion, null hypothesis, alternative, conditions, test statistic.

Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.skill: 6.11 Carrying Out a Test for the Difference of Two Population Proportions
Inference for Categorical Data: Proporti.prompt: This topic covers carrying out and concluding a two-proportion z-test for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-6.I, VAR-6.J, VAR-6.K, DAT-3.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.11). After setup: compute the pooled proportion p-hat_c, the test statistic z, and the p-value from the standard normal distribution. Compare p-value to alpha and draw a conclusion in context. Key steps: (1) state hypotheses and check conditions (Topic 6.10); (2) compute z = (p-hat_1-p-hat_2)/SE with pooled SE; (3) find p-value; (4) conclude: 'Since p-value [< or >] alpha, we [reject/fail to reject] H0. There is [convincing/not convincing] evidence that [Ha in context].' Students should carry out the complete procedure and communicate results clearly. AP exam note: all four steps (hypotheses, conditions, calculations, conclusion) must be present for full credit on free-response questions. Vocabulary: two-proportion z-test, pooled proportion, test statistic, p-value, conclusion, reject, fail to reject, context.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.1 Introducing Statistics: Should I Worry About Error?
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic introduces inference for means for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.I, Unit 7, Topic 7.1). When the population standard deviation sigma is unknown (typical in practice), we use the sample standard deviation s and the t-distribution instead of the z-distribution. Key concepts: the t-distribution is similar to the normal but has heavier tails (more probability in the extremes), reflecting the additional uncertainty from estimating sigma; as the sample size increases, the t-distribution approaches the normal; degrees of freedom (df = n-1 for one-sample) determine which t-distribution to use. This topic motivates why t-procedures replace z-procedures for means. AP exam note: students should understand the conceptual difference between z- and t-procedures and when each is appropriate (z for proportions, t for means). Vocabulary: t-distribution, degrees of freedom, sample standard deviation, heavier tails, uncertainty, z vs. t, inference.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.2 Constructing a Confidence Interval for a Population Mean
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic covers one-sample t-intervals for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.K, UNC-4.L, UNC-4.M, Unit 7, Topic 7.2). The CI for mu is x-bar +/- t* * (s/sqrt(n)) with df = n-1. Conditions: random sample, Normal/Large Sample (population normal or n >= 30 by CLT; for 15 < n < 30, check for strong skewness/outliers), 10% condition. Key concepts: t* is the critical value from the t-distribution with df = n-1; the margin of error is t* * s/sqrt(n); wider intervals mean more confidence but less precision. Students should construct and interpret CIs in context. AP exam note: interpretation: 'We are C% confident that the true mean [context] is between [lower] and [upper].' Check conditions explicitly, especially normality via graph inspection. Vocabulary: t-interval, margin of error, critical value, degrees of freedom, confidence level, conditions, interpret.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.3 Justifying a Claim About a Population Mean Based on a Confidence Interval
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic covers using t-intervals to justify claims about population means for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.N, Unit 7, Topic 7.3). If the claimed value mu0 is within the CI, the data is consistent with the claim; if outside, there is evidence against it. Key concepts: a CI provides a range of plausible values for the parameter; values outside the CI are implausible at the given confidence level; this connects CIs to hypothesis tests (if mu0 is not in the 95% CI, a two-sided test at alpha = 0.05 would reject H0: mu = mu0). Students should use CIs to evaluate claims and articulate their reasoning. AP exam note: students should clearly state whether the claimed value falls inside or outside the interval and draw the appropriate conclusion in context. Vocabulary: confidence interval, claim, plausible, implausible, evidence, hypothesis test connection, justification.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.4 Setting Up a Test for a Population Mean
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic covers setting up a one-sample t-test for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-7.A, VAR-7.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.4). Steps: (1) hypotheses: H0: mu = mu0 vs. Ha: mu < mu0, mu > mu0, or mu != mu0; (2) conditions: random, Normal/Large Sample, 10%; (3) identify as a one-sample t-test. The test statistic: t = (x-bar - mu0)/(s/sqrt(n)) with df = n-1. Key concepts: hypotheses use mu (not x-bar); one-sided vs. two-sided Ha depends on the research question; conditions must be checked before computing. Students should set up the complete test framework from contextual descriptions. AP exam note: hypotheses must be stated in terms of the parameter mu with context. Conditions should be verified with justification (e.g., describing graph shape for normality). Vocabulary: one-sample t-test, null hypothesis, alternative hypothesis, test statistic, degrees of freedom, conditions.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.5 Carrying Out a Test for a Population Mean
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic covers carrying out a one-sample t-test for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-7.C, DAT-3.E, Unit 7, Topic 7.5). After setup: compute t = (x-bar - mu0)/(s/sqrt(n)), find the p-value from the t-distribution with df = n-1, compare to alpha, and conclude in context. Key concepts: the t-distribution accounts for using s instead of sigma; for one-sided Ha: p-value = P(T >= |t|) or P(T <= -|t|); for two-sided Ha: p-value = 2*P(T >= |t|). Conclusion template: 'Since p-value [<= or >] alpha, we [reject/fail to reject] H0. There is [convincing/not convincing] evidence that [Ha in context].' AP exam note: all four components (hypotheses, conditions, calculations, conclusion) must be present. Use the t-distribution, not z, for means. Vocabulary: t-test, test statistic, p-value, t-distribution, degrees of freedom, conclude, reject, fail to reject.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.6 Confidence Intervals for the Difference of Two Means
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic covers two-sample t-intervals for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.O, UNC-4.P, UNC-4.Q, UNC-4.R, Unit 7, Topic 7.6). The CI for mu1-mu2 is (x-bar_1-x-bar_2) +/- t* * sqrt(s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2) using the t-distribution (df from technology or conservative df = min(n_1,n_2)-1). Conditions: random for both, Normal/Large Sample for both, 10% for both, independent groups. Key concepts: this CI estimates the true difference in means; if 0 is not in the interval, the means are significantly different. AP exam note: interpretation references the difference: 'We are 95% confident that the true difference in mean [context] is between [lower] and [upper].' Check all conditions for both groups. Vocabulary: two-sample t-interval, difference in means, degrees of freedom, standard error, conditions, independent.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.7 Justifying a Claim About the Difference of Two Means Based on a Confidence Interval
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic covers using two-sample t-intervals to justify claims for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.S, UNC-4.T, Unit 7, Topic 7.7). If the CI for mu1-mu2 contains 0: no convincing evidence of a difference. If entirely positive: evidence mu1 > mu2. If entirely negative: evidence mu1 < mu2. Key concepts: the CI gives both the direction and magnitude of the difference; this provides more information than a hypothesis test alone. Students should interpret two-sample CIs in context and use them to evaluate claims about differences in population means. AP exam note: clearly state whether 0 is in the interval and what this implies about the relationship between the two population means. Vocabulary: confidence interval, difference in means, contains zero, direction, magnitude, evidence, claim.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.8 Setting Up a Test for the Difference of Two Population Means
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic covers setting up a two-sample t-test for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-7.D, VAR-7.E, VAR-7.F, Unit 7, Topic 7.8). Hypotheses: H0: mu1 = mu2 (or mu1-mu2 = 0) vs. Ha: mu1 < mu2, mu1 > mu2, or mu1 != mu2. Conditions: random for both samples, Normal/Large Sample for both, 10% for both, independent groups. Test statistic: t = (x-bar_1-x-bar_2-0)/sqrt(s_1^2/n_1+s_2^2/n_2) with df from technology or conservative estimate. Key concepts: this compares two independent groups; do NOT pool standard deviations (unlike two-proportion tests). Students should write hypotheses with parameters and check all conditions. AP exam note: hypotheses use mu1 and mu2, not x-bar. Conditions must be checked for each sample. Vocabulary: two-sample t-test, null, alternative, conditions, independent groups, test statistic, degrees of freedom.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.9 Carrying Out a Test for the Difference of Two Population Means
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic covers carrying out a two-sample t-test for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-7.G, VAR-7.H, VAR-7.I, DAT-3.F, Unit 7, Topic 7.9). Compute the test statistic t and p-value using the t-distribution with appropriate df. Compare p-value to alpha and conclude in context. Key concepts: use technology for exact df (Welch's approximation) or conservative df = min(n1,n2)-1; the p-value reflects how extreme the observed difference is under H0. Conclusion: 'Since p-value [<=/>] alpha, we [reject/fail to reject] H0. There is [convincing/not convincing] evidence that [Ha in context].' Students should present the complete four-step procedure. AP exam note: all four steps required for full credit. Technology output should be referenced with parameters clearly stated. Vocabulary: two-sample t-test, Welch's df, p-value, conclusion, reject, fail to reject, context, convincing evidence.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.skill: 7.10 Skills Focus: Selecting, Implementing, and Communicating Inference Procedures
Inference for Quantitative Data: Means.prompt: This topic covers selecting appropriate inference procedures for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-3.G, DAT-3.H, Unit 7, Topic 7.10). Students must choose the correct procedure based on the research question and data type: one-sample vs. two-sample, proportions vs. means, CI vs. test. Key decision factors: number of groups (one or two), variable type (categorical = proportion, quantitative = mean), parameter of interest, and whether estimating (CI) or testing a claim (hypothesis test). Students should communicate statistical findings clearly: state the procedure, check conditions, show calculations, and interpret results in context. AP exam note: the investigative task (FRQ #6) often requires selecting and justifying a procedure. Students must explain why the chosen procedure is appropriate and present a complete analysis. Vocabulary: procedure selection, one-sample, two-sample, proportion, mean, confidence interval, hypothesis test, communication.

Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.skill: 8.1 Introducing Statistics: Are My Results Unexpected?
Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.prompt: This topic introduces chi-square inference for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-8.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.1). Chi-square tests assess whether observed categorical data differs significantly from expected patterns. Key concepts: the chi-square statistic measures the overall discrepancy between observed and expected counts; large chi-square values suggest the data does not fit the expected pattern; the chi-square distribution is right-skewed, with shape determined by degrees of freedom. This framework applies to three tests: goodness of fit (one variable, one population), homogeneity (one variable, multiple populations), and independence (two variables, one population). AP exam note: students should understand that chi-square tests compare observed vs. expected counts and that larger chi-square values indicate greater discrepancy. Vocabulary: chi-square, observed counts, expected counts, discrepancy, chi-square distribution, degrees of freedom, right-skewed.

Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.skill: 8.2 Setting Up a Chi-Square Goodness of Fit Test
Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.prompt: This topic covers setting up a chi-square goodness of fit test for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-8.B, UNC-6.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.2). This test determines whether a categorical variable's distribution matches a claimed/expected distribution. Hypotheses: H0: the distribution matches the claimed proportions; Ha: the distribution does not match. Conditions: random sample, all expected counts >= 5, independent observations. Expected counts: E_i = n * p_i where p_i is the claimed proportion for category i. The test statistic: chi^2 = sum((O_i - E_i)^2/E_i) with df = (number of categories - 1). AP exam note: hypotheses should state the specific claimed distribution. Expected counts must all be at least 5. The test is always one-sided (right tail) because large chi-square values indicate poor fit. Vocabulary: goodness of fit, expected counts, observed counts, proportions, hypotheses, degrees of freedom, chi-square.

Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.skill: 8.3 Carrying Out a Chi-Square Test for Goodness of Fit
Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.prompt: This topic covers carrying out a chi-square GOF test for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-6.B, DAT-4.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.3). After setup: compute expected counts, calculate chi^2 = sum((O-E)^2/E), find p-value using the chi-square distribution with df = k-1 (k = number of categories), and conclude. Key concepts: identify which categories contribute most to chi-square (large (O-E)^2/E values); the chi-square test is always right-tailed; a small p-value suggests the data does not follow the claimed distribution. Students should present the complete procedure and interpret which categories deviate most from expected. AP exam note: show the computation of at least one term of the chi-square statistic. Identify categories with the largest contributions. Conclude in context. Vocabulary: chi-square test, goodness of fit, contributions, p-value, right-tailed, conclude, expected, observed.

This topic covers computing expected counts in two-way tables for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-6.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.4). For chi-square tests of homogeneity or independence, the expected count for each cell is: E = (row total * column total) / grand total. This formula calculates what the counts would be if the variables were independent (or distributions were homogeneous). Key concepts: expected counts represent the 'no association' pattern; comparing observed to expected reveals where the association is strongest; all expected counts should be at least 5 for the chi-square approximation to be valid. Students should compute expected counts for any two-way table and compare them to observed counts. AP exam note: students must show the formula and compute expected counts. This skill is essential for chi-square tests of homogeneity and independence. Vocabulary: expected count, two-way table, row total, column total, grand total, independence, homogeneity.

Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.skill: 8.5 Setting Up a Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity or Independence
Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.prompt: This topic covers setting up chi-square tests for homogeneity and independence for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-6.D, UNC-6.E, Unit 8, Topic 8.5). Homogeneity: H0: the distribution of a categorical variable is the same across populations; Ha: distributions differ. Independence: H0: two categorical variables are independent; Ha: they are associated. Both use the same chi-square statistic and calculation. Conditions: random, all expected counts >= 5, independent observations. df = (r-1)(c-1) where r = rows, c = columns. Key distinction: homogeneity compares distributions across groups (data from separate samples); independence tests association within one sample. AP exam note: students must correctly identify whether the test is for homogeneity or independence based on the study design and state hypotheses accordingly. Vocabulary: homogeneity, independence, two-way table, distribution, association, degrees of freedom, chi-square.

Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.skill: 8.6 Carrying Out a Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity or Independence
Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.prompt: This topic covers carrying out chi-square tests for homogeneity/independence for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-6.F, DAT-4.B, Unit 8, Topic 8.6). Compute expected counts, chi^2 = sum((O-E)^2/E) with df = (r-1)(c-1), find p-value from chi-square distribution, and conclude. Key concepts: identify cells with the largest contributions to chi-square; describe the nature of the association (which categories are over- or under-represented); the test is always right-tailed. Students should present the complete procedure and interpret results, noting which cells contribute most and what the pattern suggests. AP exam note: after concluding, describe the nature of the association based on which cells have the largest (O-E)^2/E values and the direction of O-E. Vocabulary: chi-square test, homogeneity, independence, contributions, p-value, association, pattern, conclude.

Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.skill: 8.7 Skills Focus: Selecting an Appropriate Inference Procedure for Categorical Data
Inference for Categorical Data: Chi-Squa.prompt: This topic covers selecting among chi-square procedures for AP Statistics (CED: DAT-4.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.7). Students choose between: one-proportion z-test/interval (one categorical variable, one or two categories), two-proportion z-test/interval (comparing two proportions), chi-square GOF (one variable, testing against a specific distribution), chi-square homogeneity (comparing distributions across groups), chi-square independence (testing association between two variables in one sample). Decision factors: number of variables, number of groups, type of claim. Students should justify their procedure choice based on the research question and data structure. AP exam note: the investigative task may require selecting among these procedures. Students must explain why the chosen procedure is appropriate for the given scenario. Vocabulary: procedure selection, chi-square, z-test, goodness of fit, homogeneity, independence, proportions, categorical.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.skill: 9.1 Introducing Statistics: Do Those Points Align?
Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.prompt: This topic introduces inference for regression slopes for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-9.A, Unit 9, Topic 9.1). In simple linear regression, the sample slope b estimates the population slope beta (the true linear relationship between x and y). Due to sampling variability, different samples produce different slopes. The question: is the observed linear relationship real (beta != 0) or just due to chance (beta = 0)? Key concepts: the regression model is y = alpha + beta*x + epsilon, where epsilon is normally distributed error with mean 0 and constant SD sigma; inference for slope uses the t-distribution. This topic motivates confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for beta. AP exam note: students should understand that inference for slopes determines whether a linear relationship exists in the population, not just the sample. Vocabulary: regression slope, population slope beta, sample slope b, sampling variability, linear model, error term.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.skill: 9.2 Confidence Intervals for the Slope of a Regression Model
Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.prompt: This topic covers confidence intervals for the regression slope for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.U, UNC-4.V, UNC-4.W, Unit 9, Topic 9.2). The CI for beta is b +/- t* * SE_b with df = n-2, where SE_b is the standard error of the slope (from computer output). Conditions: Linear (scatterplot shows linear pattern), Independent (observations independent, 10% condition), Normal (residuals approximately normal -- check residual plot and normal probability plot), Equal variance (residual plot shows constant spread) -- LINE conditions. Key concepts: the CI gives a range of plausible values for the true slope; SE_b is typically read from regression output. AP exam note: conditions must be checked using residual plots and normal probability plots. The CI should be interpreted in context: 'We are 95% confident that for each additional [x-unit], the true mean [y] changes by between [lower] and [upper] [y-units].' Vocabulary: confidence interval, slope, standard error, t-distribution, LINE conditions, residual plot, degrees of freedom.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.skill: 9.3 Justifying a Claim About the Slope of a Regression Model Based on a Confidence Interval
Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.prompt: This topic covers using CIs for slope to justify claims for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.X, UNC-4.Y, Unit 9, Topic 9.3). If the CI for beta does not contain 0, there is evidence of a linear relationship; if it contains 0, there is not convincing evidence. Key concepts: if the entire CI is positive, there is evidence of a positive linear relationship; if entirely negative, a negative relationship; the width of the CI indicates precision of the slope estimate. Students should connect CI results to the corresponding hypothesis test conclusion. AP exam note: students should state whether 0 is in the CI and draw the appropriate conclusion about the existence and direction of the linear relationship in context. Vocabulary: confidence interval, slope, claim, contains zero, linear relationship, direction, evidence, justification.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.skill: 9.4 Setting Up a Test for the Slope of a Regression Model
Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.prompt: This topic covers setting up a t-test for regression slope for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.Z, UNC-4.AA, Unit 9, Topic 9.4). Hypotheses: H0: beta = 0 (no linear relationship) vs. Ha: beta < 0, beta > 0, or beta != 0. Conditions: LINE (Linear, Independent, Normal residuals, Equal variance). Test statistic: t = b/SE_b with df = n-2. Key concepts: the null hypothesis beta = 0 means x has no linear effect on y; the test statistic and SE_b are typically read from computer regression output; students should be able to identify these values from output tables (look for the slope row). AP exam note: hypotheses use beta (not b), stated in context. Students must check LINE conditions using residual plots. Vocabulary: t-test for slope, null hypothesis, alternative, beta, test statistic, standard error, LINE conditions.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.skill: 9.5 Carrying Out a Test for the Slope of a Regression Model
Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.prompt: This topic covers carrying out a t-test for slope for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-4.AB, DAT-3.I, Unit 9, Topic 9.5). Compute t = b/SE_b (or read from output), find p-value from t-distribution with df = n-2, compare to alpha, and conclude. Key concepts: the p-value tests whether the observed slope could have occurred by chance if beta = 0; a small p-value provides evidence of a real linear relationship. Reading computer output: the t-statistic and p-value are in the coefficients table, typically in the row for the explanatory variable. Students should present the complete four-step procedure. AP exam note: students must identify the correct values from computer output, show the test setup, and conclude in context. The conclusion should reference both the slope and the variables involved. Vocabulary: t-test for slope, p-value, computer output, t-statistic, degrees of freedom, conclude, linear relationship.

Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.skill: 9.6 Skills Focus: Selecting an Appropriate Inference Procedure
Inference for Quantitative Data: Slopes.prompt: This topic covers selecting the appropriate inference procedure across all AP Statistics topics (CED: DAT-3.J, Unit 9, Topic 9.6). Students must choose among all learned procedures: one-sample z (proportion CI/test), two-sample z (proportion CI/test), one-sample t (mean CI/test), two-sample t (mean CI/test), chi-square GOF, chi-square homogeneity/independence, and linear regression t (slope CI/test). Decision factors: (1) What type of variable? Categorical = proportions/chi-square; quantitative = means/regression. (2) How many groups/variables? One, two, or association. (3) CI or test? Estimating vs. testing a claim. Students should justify their choice and implement the complete procedure. AP exam note: the investigative task requires selecting and justifying the appropriate procedure. Students must clearly explain why the procedure fits the data and question. This is the capstone skill of the course. Vocabulary: procedure selection, proportions, means, chi-square, regression, confidence interval, hypothesis test, justify, communicate.
